question: Explain Provider: ChangeNotifier, Consumer, context.watch vs context.read vs context.select.
answer:
Provider wraps InheritedWidget with a simpler API for DI and state management.

Core components:
• ChangeNotifier — mutable state class; call notifyListeners() to trigger rebuilds
• ChangeNotifierProvider — inserts ChangeNotifier into the tree
• Consumer<T> — rebuilds only its builder subtree when T notifies
• context.watch<T>() — subscribe; rebuilds the CURRENT widget when T notifies
• context.read<T>() — one-time read, NO subscription (for callbacks)
• context.select<T,R>() — subscribe to a specific slice; rebuilds only when that slice changes

Rule: read() in callbacks, watch()/Consumer in build().

question: Explain ProxyProvider and how to combine providers.
answer:
ProxyProvider creates a provider that depends on other providers. It rebuilds when its dependencies change.

Use cases:
• A service that needs a dependency injected at runtime
• A provider whose value is derived from another provider
• Repository that needs an authenticated HTTP client

ProxyProvider variants:
• ProxyProvider<A, T> — depends on 1 provider
• ProxyProvider2<A, B, T> — depends on 2 providers
• ChangeNotifierProxyProvider — produces a ChangeNotifier that depends on others

question: What is the difference between Provider.of, Consumer, and Selector?
answer:
Three ways to read data from Provider:

1. Provider.of<T>(context)
   • Direct access
   • Rebuilds widget when data changes
   • listen: false to disable rebuilds

2. Consumer<T>
   • Widget that rebuilds
   • Only child subtree rebuilds
   • More granular control

3. Selector<A, S>
   • Rebuilds only when selected value changes
   • Better performance
   • Useful for large objects

context.watch<T>() — equivalent to Provider.of
context.read<T>() — equivalent to Provider.of with listen: false

question: Interview scenario #10: How would you handle code review readiness for code generation in a Flutter app?
answer:
Submit small focused changes, document non-obvious decisions, and include tests that prove behavior before and after.

Focus area: code generation. In interviews, explain trade-offs, failure modes, and how you would validate the result in production.

question: Interview scenario #33: How would you handle state management trade-offs for memory leak prevention in a Flutter app?
answer:
Choose the simplest solution that fits scale: local state for small screens, Provider/Riverpod/BLoC for shared or asynchronous flows.

Focus area: memory leak prevention. In interviews, explain trade-offs, failure modes, and how you would validate the result in production.

question: Interview scenario #56: How would you handle API integration for accessibility in a Flutter app?
answer:
Model API responses with typed DTOs, validate nullable fields, and centralize request/retry logic in a data layer.

Focus area: accessibility. In interviews, explain trade-offs, failure modes, and how you would validate the result in production.

question: Interview scenario #79: How would you handle stream handling for analytics instrumentation in a Flutter app?
answer:
Cancel subscriptions in dispose, debounce noisy sources, and keep transformation logic in dedicated services or blocs.

Focus area: analytics instrumentation. In interviews, explain trade-offs, failure modes, and how you would validate the result in production.

question: Interview scenario #102: How would you handle performance optimization for BuildContext usage in a Flutter app?
answer:
Measure first with DevTools, remove unnecessary rebuilds, cache expensive operations, and prefer const constructors where possible.

Focus area: BuildContext usage. In interviews, explain trade-offs, failure modes, and how you would validate the result in production.
